title: Steady with ADHD
description: A comprehensive 8-week program designed to help adults with ADHD develop sustainable strategies for focus, emotional regulation, time management, and daily productivity.

modules.title: Welcome & Intake
modules.subtitle: Week 1
modules.summary: Complete your Steady Assessments  and learn what to expect from the Steady with ADHD program.

content.rawContent:
Welcome to the Steady with ADHD program! This program is designed specifically for adults living with ADHD who want to build sustainable strategies for managing their symptoms and thriving in daily life.

Over the next 8 weeks, you'll work through modules covering self-activation, emotional regulation, time management, productivity, and more. Each module includes educational content, practical strategies, and steady work to help you apply what you learn.

Here's what you can expect:

Weekly modules with videos to convey the concepts
Steady strategy cards with practical techniques you can use right away
Journal prompts to reflect on your progress
Steady work assignments to practice new skills
Weekly meetings with your clinician
The goal is sustainable change, not perfection.
content.styledHtml:
Welcome to the Steady with ADHD Program!

This program is designed specifically for adults living with ADHD who want to build sustainable strategies for managing their symptoms and thriving in daily life.

Over the next 8 weeks, you'll work through modules covering focus, emotional regulation, time management, productivity, and more. Each module includes educational content, practical strategies, and steady work to help you apply what you learn.

What You Can Expect

  • Weekly modules with videos to convey the concepts
  • Steady strategy cards with practical techniques you can use right aways
  • Journal prompts to reflect on your progress 
  • Steady work assignments to practice new skills
  • Weekly meetings with your clinician

Remember: Take your time with each module — there's no rush. The goal is sustainable change, not perfection.

Setting Yourself Up for Success

To get the most out of our sessions together, here's what we've found works best:

Your Session Environment

  • 📍 Find your focus zone: Attend from a private, productive space

  • 🎧 Use headphones: This helps you stay focused and engaged
  • 💻 Have your laptop ready: You'll need it for activities and resources

Your Weekly Commitment

📚 Complete 1 hour per week of Steady Work — This keeps the momentum going between our sessions

📋 Bring your Steady System (paper or digital) to every meeting — This is your personal toolkit for success

🎯 Important: Your Action Signal

Create SMART Intended Outcomes

S - Subjective

Define what success looks and feels like to you personally. What would meaningful progress mean in your daily life?

M - Measurable

Identify specific metrics or indicators you can track. How will you know you're making progress?

A - Achievable

Ensure your goals are realistic and attainable given your current circumstances and resources.

R - Relevant

Align your outcomes with your personal values and current life priorities. Why do these goals matter to you?

T - Time-Bound

Set clear deadlines and milestones. When do you want to achieve these outcomes?

Current Challenges to Address

Identify 3 key challenges you'd like your intended outcomes to help address:

  • Challenge 1: ________________________
  • Challenge 2: ________________________
  • Challenge 3: ________________________

🎯 Steady Task: Capture your intended outcomes in your Pre-Steady System, then integrate them into your Steady System once established.

content.instructions: Based on the challenges you've identified, let's create specific, achievable goals that will help you make meaningful progress. Think about what concrete steps you can take to address each challenge. Your goals should be Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound (SMART). Consider starting with smaller, manageable goals that build momentum toward larger changes.

parts.title: Understanding Memory & ADHD - Preparing for Steady Tasks

content.rawContent:
PART 4: Memory

ADHD includes dysfunction in prospective and working memory.

Prospective memory: The ability to remember to do something in the future at the right time.

Working Memory: The ability to store information in the moment.

In Part 5, you'll be assigned several Steady Tasks to complete before our next meeting. I expect you to capture each Steady Task in writing during our meetings. If it isn't recorded in your system, it's unlikely to happen — and consistent implementation is required to continue the program.

Until your full Steady System is built, use your Pre-Steady System to track these tasks. Open the Notes app or use the paper where you drafted your intended outcomes earlier.
content.styledHtml:
Understanding Memory & ADHD

ADHD includes dysfunction in prospective and working memory — two critical systems that affect daily functioning.

Prospective Memory

The ability to remember to do something in the future at the right time

Working Memory

The ability to store information in the moment while using it

Preparing for Steady Tasks

In Part 5, you'll be assigned several Steady Tasks to complete before our next meeting.

⚠️ Critical Success Factor: I expect you to capture each Steady Task in writing during our meetings.

If it isn't recorded in your system, it's unlikely to happen — and consistent implementation is required to continue the program.

Action Required Now

Until your full Steady System is built, use your Pre-Steady System to track these tasks:

  • Open the Notes app on your device
  • Or use the paper where you drafted your intended outcomes earlier
  • Have it ready to capture every Steady Task assigned in Part 5

cards.body: When a thought pops into your head, you have two choices: act on it immediately or capture it in writing. Don't trust your brain to remember it later – it's already moved on to the next thing! Keep a notepad, phone app, or sticky notes handy for quick captures.
cards.title: Do It Now or Write It Down

cards.body: Need to remember to take something with you? Place it directly in your path where you can't miss it. Put your keys on top of the papers you need, or place your water bottle by the door. Make forgetting impossible by creating physical reminders.
cards.title: Put the Thing in the Way So The Thing You Will Take

cards.body: If you read a text, email, or message but can't respond right away, immediately mark it as unread. This creates a visual reminder that action is still needed. Your future self will thank you when that important message doesn't get lost in the shuffle.
cards.title: If The Response You'll Forget, Mark As Unread

cards.body: Don't pretend you'll remember – you won't, and that's okay! Set alarms for everything: appointments, medication, leaving the house, taking breaks. Give yourself buffer time and use specific alarm labels so you know exactly what each one is for.
cards.title: Alarms Are Your Friend, You'll Forget, Don't Pretend

cards.body: When doing multi-step tasks, narrate each step as you complete it: 'I'm turning off the stove, now I'm grabbing my keys, now I'm locking the door.' This helps you stay present and remember what comes next in the sequence.
cards.title: Speak Aloud The Steps So You Don't Forget What's Next

cards.body: Every important item needs a designated home: keys by the door, wallet in the same pocket, glasses on the nightstand. When you use something, always return it to its spot immediately. Consistency is key – pick logical locations and stick to them religiously.
cards.title: Give It a Spot or Lose It a Lot

PART 2: GET UP TO SPEED
Before building forward, we finish what's undone
content.styledHtml:
Before building forward, we finish what's undone

Where Are You Behind?

Take a moment to honestly assess what's been piling up. Naming it helps us tackle it systematically.

  • 📧 Emails — unread messages, delayed responses
  • 💬 Messages — texts, calls, social media
  • 🧹 Cleaning — dishes, laundry, organization
  • 💼 Work tasks — projects, deadlines, follow-ups
  • 💳 Bills — payments, financial paperwork
  • 📋 Administrative tasks — appointments, forms, documentation
  • 🤝 Other incomplete commitments — promises made, plans started

⏰ Step 1: Estimate Completion Time

Getting realistic about time helps prevent overwhelm and creates achievable plans.

✅ Estimate duration for each task
Be honest about how long things actually take, not how long you wish they took.

🔍 Compare to similar past tasks
Think about the last time you did something similar. How long did it really take?

🎬 Visualize the steps
If duration feels unclear, mentally walk through each step from start to finish.

⏱️ Time a small portion
For big tasks, time yourself doing a small part to create a realistic baseline.

🗂️ Step 2: Choose Your Catch-Up Structure

Pick the approach that feels most doable for your current energy and schedule.

📝 Write everything down
Get the full picture by listing all incomplete tasks in one place.

📅 Schedule in small blocks
Spread tasks across multiple days in manageable chunks.

👥 Body doubling session
Schedule catch-up work during a virtual co-working or accountability session.

🎯 Focused block method
Dedicate one longer time period and power through everything at once.

🔄 Protected reset time
Take time off specifically to catch up and create a fresh start.

💡 Remember: The goal isn't perfection — it's progress. Choose the approach that feels sustainable and gets you moving forward.

parts.title: Steady Work: Get Up to Speed

items.description: Identify What You're Behind On — Make a list of tasks you need to catch up on. Examples: Emails, Texts, Cleaning, Work tasks, Bills, Chores.

Break Each Item Into Smaller Steps — Divide big tasks into manageable parts. Examples: Work emails vs. personal emails; list chores and start with the easiest; list bills and start by paying one or setting up auto-pay.
Estimate How Long Each Step Will Take — Write down an approximate time for each step. Being realistic helps you plan without overwhelm.

Schedule Each Step in Your STEADY SYSTEM — Break tasks into hours or blocks. Be realistic about what you can complete in one sitting. Consider using the Pomodoro Method (25 minutes work / 5 minutes break). Calculate your total estimated time to catch up.
Start Catching Up — Begin with your first step. Focus on progress, not perfection.

Bring the Future to the Present: Visualize yourself fully caught up. Use all your senses — see it, hear it, feel it. Imagine how you'll feel when the last task is done. Describe your experience of finishing these tasks.

content.styledHtml:
ADHD is fundamentally a difference in how the brain regulates attention, motivation, and executive function. Understanding this is the foundation for everything else in this program.

The Dopamine Connection The ADHD brain has differences in dopamine regulation — the neurotransmitter responsible for motivation, reward, and focus. This isn't a character flaw; it's neurobiology. When a task doesn't provide enough stimulation, the ADHD brain struggles to engage — not because  lazy, but because the neurochemical reward system works differently.

Executive Function Challenges Executive functions are the brain's management system. They include:

  • Working memory — holding information in mind while using it
  • Cognitive flexibility — shifting between tasks or perspectives
  • Inhibitory control — stopping impulses and filtering distractions
  • Planning & organization — breaking goals into steps
  • Time perception — estimating and tracking time

With ADHD, these functions are inconsistent — not absent. You might excel at planning a vacation but struggle to plan your workday. This inconsistency is a hallmark of ADHD.

Interest-Based Nervous System Dr. William Dodson describes the ADHD motivation system as "interest-based" rather than "importance-based." You're motivated by:

  • Novelty — new and stimulating things
  • Challenge — competitive or urgent situations
  • Interest — things that genuinely fascinate you
  • Urgency — deadlines and time pressure

Understanding this helps explain why you can hyperfocus on a hobby for 6 hours but can't start a 15-minute work task.

parts.title: Bring the Future to the Present: Completion Visualization

prompts: Close your eyes and imagine yourself fully caught up on all your tasks. What do you see around you? What does your space look like when everything is complete?
How does your body feel when that last task is finished? What physical sensations do you notice - in your shoulders, your breathing, your posture?
What sounds do you hear in this moment of completion? Is it quiet? Are there celebratory sounds? What's the atmosphere like?
Describe the emotional experience of being completely caught up. What feelings come up? Relief? Pride? Peace? Something else?
What would you say to yourself in this moment of completion? What words of acknowledgment or celebration feel right?
How might this feeling of completion ripple into other areas of your life? What becomes possible when you're caught up?

cards.body: When you can't start a task, your brain isn't getting enough dopamine from it. Try adding music, body doubling, or a small reward to boost stimulation.
cards.title: Not Lazy — Understimulated

cards.body: Your working memory is juggling too much. Externalize your thoughts: write it down, set reminders, use visual cues.
cards.title: Not Forgetful — Overloaded

cards.body: Standard organization systems often fail for ADHD brains. Build systems around visibility — if you can't see it, it doesn't exist.
cards.title: Not Disorganized — Differently Organized

cards.body: Your motivation follows interest and novelty, not importance. Work with this by rotating tasks, gamifying boring ones, or pairing them with something enjoyable.
cards.title: Not Inconsistent — Interest-Driven

cards.body: Emotional intensity is part of ADHD. Rejection Sensitive Dysphoria (RSD) is real. Recognize the pattern: strong emotion → pause → respond (not react).
cards.title: Not Oversensitive — Deeply Feeling

prompts: What was your reaction to learning about the interest-based nervous system? Does it resonate with your experience?
Think of a time when you hyperfocused on something. What made that activity so engaging?
What's one area of your life where you'd most like to see improvement through this program?

content.rawContent:
Work Review Continuation: What Got in the Way?

Reflecting on barriers and obstacles is crucial for improving your work approach. Consider what didn't work, where you stalled, what barriers appeared, and what needs adjusting for next time. We study these barriers so we can push through them more effectively.

Getting Specific About Progress:
It's important to measure progress objectively, not based on feelings but on actual numbers and concrete outcomes. Ask yourself: How much time did you actually work? What did that time produce? What work remains? How long will the remaining tasks realistically take?

Steady Task: Review what's left and ensure everything is clearly documented in your Steady System for better organization and follow-through.
content.styledHtml:
Work Review: What Got in the Way?

Barrier Analysis

Let's examine what didn't work:

  • What didn't work as planned?
  • Where did you stall or get stuck?
  • What barriers showed up unexpectedly?
  • What will need adjusting next time?

💡 Remember: We study barriers so we can push through them more effectively next time.

Get Specific: What Actually Got Done?

Base your assessment on facts, not feelings. Focus on measurable outcomes:

  • Time Investment: How much time did you actually work?
  • Output: What did that time produce?
  • Remaining Work: What still needs to be completed?
  • Realistic Timeline: How long will the rest realistically take?

📋 Steady Task

Review what's left and make sure it is clearly written down inside your Steady System. Clear documentation is key to staying organized and following through on your commitments.

content.rawContent:
This is where your Steady System matters

Your Steady System is not for catching up. It is for staying current.

It holds what your brain cannot reliably hold. It reduces future overwhelm. It protects your energy.

Build it now so you don't have to rescue yourself later. More planning now creates more flow later.

Plan when you are calm and in preparation mode. Not when you are rushing or reacting.

Preparation protects momentum. Planning ahead reduces decision fatigue. Deciding in advance keeps you steady when the day gets busy.

Build structure when you have space. Let that structure carry you when you don't.

content.rawContent:
Programming means a task becomes automatic through repetition.

When something is programmed:
• It requires less thought.
• It requires less energy.
• You are less likely to forget.
• It is more likely to happen.

Examples:
• Brushing teeth
• Making lunch
• Work preparation
• Evening reset

Repetition builds reliability.

Schedule System Check-Ins

Your Steady System only works if you look at it. Program the review.

• Daily check-in (morning works well for most people)
• Weekly review (often Sunday)

Regular review increases awareness. Awareness increases stability. Stability builds confidence.

parts.title: Daily To-Do Lists & Time Blocking for ADHD Success

content.rawContent:
Use a To-Do List Daily

Your list is external memory.

Identify steady tasks and write them down.
Keep both long-term and short-term items.
If something is no longer relevant, remove it.
Let the list stay current and active.

Crossing something off is not trivial.
Completion provides dopamine.

Daily To-Do Lists & Time Blocking for ADHD Success

Your list is external memory.

✨ The Power of Completion

content.styledHtml:
Attention in ADHD isn't broken — it's dysregulated. You have plenty of attention; the challenge is directing it intentionally. This module gives you concrete strategies to work with your brain's natural patterns.

The Attention Spectrum ADHD attention isn't binary (focused vs. unfocused). It exists on a spectrum:

  • Hyperfocus — deep, consuming engagement (hard to stop)
  • Flow state — engaged, productive, time flies
  • Normal attention — adequate focus with some drift
  • Scattered attention — jumping between things, restless
  • Attention shutdown — unable to engage at all, foggy

Your goal isn't to be in hyperfocus all the time — it's to spend more time in the flow-to-normal range and have tools for when you're scattered.

Your Peak Performance Windows Using last week's energy tracking data, you likely noticed patterns. Most people with ADHD have 2-3 hours of peak cognitive performance per day. The key insight: protect those hours ruthlessly for your most important work.

cards.body: Traditional 25-min Pomodoros are too rigid for ADHD. Try flexible intervals: work until you feel restless (10-45 min), then take a 5-min break. Track your natural rhythm over a week.
cards.title: The Pomodoro Remix

cards.body: Working alongside someone (in person or virtually) provides just enough external accountability and stimulation to keep going. Try FocusMate or ask a friend to co-work.
cards.title: Body Doubling

cards.body: Remove friction from starting: keep your workspace ready, browser tabs closed, phone in another room. Make the right thing the easy thing.
cards.title: Environment Design

cards.body: Can't start a big task? Commit to just 2 minutes. The hardest part is starting — once you're in motion, momentum often carries you forward.
cards.title: The 2-Minute Bridge

cards.body: When a task gets stale: change your location, switch to a different tool, add background music, use a different color pen, or race a timer.
cards.title: Novelty Injection

cards.body: Group similar small tasks together (all emails, all phone calls, all admin). Switching between task types costs extra energy with ADHD.
cards.title: Task Batching

prompts: How sustainable do you think it will be to consistently use your STEADY SYSTEM? Consider your current lifestyle, energy levels, and daily routines as you reflect on this.

What might be the barriers that could prevent you from maintaining your STEADY SYSTEM consistently? Think about both internal challenges (like motivation, energy, or mood) and external ones (like time constraints, environment, or other people).
What strategies could you put in place to overcome these potential barriers? How might you adapt your system when life gets challenging?
On a scale of 1-10, how confident do you feel about maintaining your STEADY SYSTEM over the next month? What would help increase that confidence?

content.styledHtml:
Self-Activation: Starting When Motivation Is Low

Self-activation is the ability to begin and continue tasks without relying on external pressure or immediate rewards.

Now that you know what to do and when to do it, the next step is learning how to start.

What Motivates the ADHD Brain

People with ADHD are often naturally activated by:

  • Novelty — new experiences and challenges
  • Interest or passion — topics that genuinely excite you
  • Competition — games, challenges, or comparison with others
  • Urgency — deadlines and time pressure
  • Intensity — high stakes or emotional investment
  • Pleasure — immediate enjoyment or satisfaction

You aren't flawed.

You need more stimulation.

When Natural Motivators Aren't Present

If those natural motivators aren't available, you can support yourself by:

Use activation strategies intentionally
Apply specific techniques to get started
Identify your procrastination patterns
Notice when and why you get stuck
Reduce unhealthy dopamine behaviors
Limit activities that provide quick hits without progress
Increase healthy dopamine behaviors
Build in rewarding activities that support your goals
Support your brain's regulation
Focus on sleep, nutrition, and movement

Remember: Activation is supported — not forced.

content.rawContent: The Perfectionist - Strives for unrealistic outcomes and fears failure to the extreme extent that they don't even start. The Avoider - They're so anxious about starting something that they use procrastination as a defense mechanism against the anxiety. The Partier - Intentionally seeks fun in order to forget about the task they've been dreading. The Plenty-of-time-er - Tells themselves "I have plenty of time to get it done" in order to delay starting. The Overly confident - Puts off a task because they believe it will be easy and quick. The Over-achiever - The task feels huge, even starting feels like too much and getting stuck means no other productivity. The Waiter - Tells themselves that they will wait to get to the emotionally heavy task once a stressful/busy time is over. Procrastination Patterns to Notice (without judgment) Naming the pattern reduces its power. Finding your style increases self-awareness.
content.styledHtml:
Understanding Your Procrastination Patterns

✨ Remember: These patterns are completely normal and recognizing them is the first step toward change. There's no judgment here — just awareness and growth.

Common Procrastination Types

🎯 The Perfectionist

Strives for unrealistic outcomes and fears failure to the extreme extent that they don't even start.

😰 The Avoider

They're so anxious about starting something that they use procrastination as a defense mechanism against the anxiety.

🎉 The Partier

Intentionally seeks fun in order to forget about the task they've been dreading.

⏰ The Plenty-of-time-er

Tells themselves "I have plenty of time to get it done" in order to delay starting.

💪 The Overly Confident

Puts off a task because they believe it will be easy and quick.

🏆 The Over-achiever

The task feels huge, even starting feels like too much and getting stuck means no other productivity.

⏳ The Waiter

Tells themselves that they will wait to get to the emotionally heavy task once a stressful/busy time is over.

🧠 Why This Matters

  • Naming the pattern reduces its power — When you can identify what's happening, you're already taking control
  • Finding your style increases self-awareness — Understanding your unique patterns helps you develop personalized strategies
  • No judgment needed — These patterns developed for good reasons, and recognizing them is a sign of growth

Which pattern(s) feel most familiar to you? Remember, many people identify with more than one type, and that's completely normal!

content.rawContent: The Last-minute-er - Gets a buzz from waiting until the last minute to complete a task and the pressure to finish helps them get started. The Novelty seeker - Puts off the task because they come up with more new and exciting ideas. The Producer - Gets anything else on their "to do list" done except the one emotionally heavy task they actually need to do. The Daydreamer - Spends time fantasizing about the tasks they hope to accomplish while putting off those exact tasks. The Denier - Pretends like they don't care about anything, but their to-do list actually eats them up inside. The Procrastivator - has to meet specific conditions in order to engage in a task ex: I need to clean my desk before I start a project. Bedtime Revenger - They feel like the day didn't include enough time for what matters to them, so at night—when things are finally quiet—they stay up late and delay sleep to get that time back. More Procrastination Patterns to Notice (without judgment). There are many ways to postpone tasks. But how do we start them?!
content.styledHtml:
ADHD Procrastination Patterns Cont.

Understanding your unique procrastination style is the first step toward working with your ADHD brain, not against it. See which patterns resonate with you:

🚀 The Last-Minute-er

Gets a buzz from waiting until the last minute to complete a task—the pressure to finish helps them get started.

✨ The Novelty Seeker

Puts off the task because they come up with more new and exciting ideas that feel more compelling in the moment.

🏃 The Producer

Gets anything else on their "to-do list" done except the one emotionally heavy task they actually need to do.

💭 The Daydreamer

Spends time fantasizing about the tasks they hope to accomplish while putting off those exact tasks.

🤷 The Denier

Pretends like they don't care about anything, but their to-do list actually eats them up inside.

⚙️ The Procrastivator

Has to meet specific conditions in order to engage in a task. Example: "I need to clean my desk before I start this project."

🌙 The Bedtime Revenger

Feels like the day didn't include enough time for what matters to them, so at night—when things are finally quiet—they stay up late and delay sleep to get that time back.

Remember: No Judgment Zone

These patterns exist for reasons that make sense to your ADHD brain. There are many ways to postpone tasks—and that's completely normal and human.

But How Do We Start Them?!

Great question! Understanding your pattern is step one. Next, we'll explore strategies tailored to work with your specific procrastination style.

content.rawContent:
Understanding What Interferes with Self-Activation

Self-activation - the ability to get yourself started on tasks and maintain momentum - can be challenging for anyone, but especially for those with ADHD. Rather than trying to force yourself through these barriers, we focus on reducing the friction that makes tasks feel impossible to start.

Common Barriers to Self-Activation:

• Time blindness - Difficulty accurately estimating how long tasks will take or sensing the passage of time
• Micro-stressors - Small, seemingly insignificant sources of stress that accumulate throughout the day
• Unhealthy dopamine habits - Relying on instant gratification activities that deplete motivation for other tasks
• Distractions - Environmental or internal interruptions that pull attention away from intended activities
• Negative self-talk - Internal criticism that creates emotional barriers to starting
• Boring or unclear tasks - Activities that lack clear structure, purpose, or engagement

Our Approach: We reduce friction rather than increase force.

Instead of pushing harder against these barriers, we work on making tasks easier to start by addressing the root causes of resistance. This might mean breaking tasks into smaller steps, creating clearer structure, or finding ways to make activities more engaging and rewarding.

content.rawContent:
PART 3 — Time Blindness and Time Horizon

Time blindness: difficulty sensing how long tasks take or how long you have been doing something.

Time horizon: difficulty sensing the distance between now and a future event.

With time blindness:
- Tasks feel longer than they are
- The future does not feel urgent
- Planning feels optional

Other patterns:
- Time accelerates during hyperfocus
- Time slows during boredom
- Preferred tasks expand
- Mundane tasks shrink or are avoided
- Switching tasks is difficult

Avoidance often leads to shame. Clarity reduces shame.
content.styledHtml:
Time Blindness and Time Horizon

Time blindness: difficulty sensing how long tasks take or how long you have been doing something.

Time horizon: difficulty sensing the distance between now and a future event.

How Time Blindness Shows Up

  • Tasks feel longer than they actually are
  • The future doesn't feel urgent or real
  • Planning feels optional or unnecessary

Other Time Patterns

  • During hyperfocus: Time accelerates and hours pass unnoticed
  • During boredom: Time slows to a crawl
  • Preferred tasks: Expand to fill available time
  • Mundane tasks: Shrink in your mind or get avoided entirely
  • Task switching: Feels difficult and disruptive

💡 Remember: Avoidance often leads to shame.
Clarity reduces shame.

Optimize the Environment:
- Reduce visual clutter

Ask yourself:

content.styledHtml:
Eliminating Micro-Stressors

Micro-stressors are small environmental irritations that increase dysregulation, consciously or unconsciously. Even minor friction affects your ability to stay regulated.

Your environment should support your brain — not drain it.

Optimize Your Environment

  • Reduce visual clutter — Clear surfaces and organized spaces reduce mental load
  • Adjust lighting — Find the right balance of natural and artificial light
  • Minimize background noise — Consider noise-canceling or white noise options
  • Improve comfort — Temperature, seating, and physical comfort matter
  • Be intentional about sensory input — Consider sight, sound, smell, and texture

Reflection Questions

  • What helps me feel calm and focused?
  • What environmental factors drain my energy?

Remember: Small changes can reduce significant stress load over time.

cards.body: Stop what you're doing. Take 3 deep breaths. Observe what you're feeling (name it). Proceed with intention. This 30-second practice creates the gap between emotion and action.
cards.title: The STOP Technique

cards.body: When you feel emotional flooding, literally say (or think) the name of the emotion: 'I'm feeling rejected' or 'This is frustration.' Naming activates the prefrontal cortex and reduces amygdala intensity.
cards.title: Name It to Tame It

cards.body: When you feel intense rejection, ask: 'What's the evidence? What would I tell a friend? Will this matter in a week?' RSD makes perceived rejection feel like actual rejection — the feeling is real, but the interpretation may not be.
cards.title: The RSD Reality Check

cards.body: When emotions overwhelm, engage your senses: hold ice, splash cold water on your face, listen to a specific song, do 10 jumping jacks. Physical sensation interrupts emotional spirals.
cards.title: Sensory Reset

cards.body: Before situations that trigger you (difficult conversations, feedback sessions), prepare: remind yourself of your values, rehearse your STOP technique, and give yourself permission to take a break if needed.
cards.title: The Emotional Buffer

content.rawContent: Make desired behaviors easier by reducing friction and increasing accessibility. Buy pre-cut fruit instead of whole fruit. Keep keys on a hook by the door. Store cleaning tools where they are used. Use visible organization rather than hidden storage. Choose tools you will realistically use (Swiffer instead of mop). Reduce steps and friction in your daily routines. Increase accessibility by clearing clutter regularly. Sort items into four groups: Keep, Donate, Sell, Discard. Remember: less excess equals less cognitive load.
content.styledHtml:
Eliminating Barriers to Success

Core Principle: Make Desired Behaviors Easier

The key to building sustainable habits is removing friction from the behaviors you want to maintain.

Practical Examples

  • Buy pre-cut fruit instead of whole fruit
  • Keys on a hook by the door
  • Cleaning tools stored where they are used
  • Visible organization rather than hidden storage
  • Use tools you will realistically use (Swiffer instead of mop)

Reduce Steps

Eliminate unnecessary actions between you and your goal

Reduce Friction

Remove obstacles that make tasks feel difficult

Increase Accessibility

Make helpful resources easy to reach and use

The Four-Group Decluttering System

Clear clutter regularly by sorting items into:

Keep
Donate
Sell
Discard

Remember: Less excess = Less cognitive load

Emotional dysregulation is one of the most impactful — and least talked about — aspects of ADHD. Research shows that up to 70% of adults with ADHD experience significant emotional regulation challenges.

Why Emotions Hit Harder with ADHD The same executive function differences that affect attention also affect emotional processing:

  • Intensity — Emotions are felt more strongly and rapidly
  • Impulsivity — Less time between feeling and reacting
  • Rumination — Difficulty letting go of negative emotions
  • Rejection Sensitive Dysphoria (RSD) — Extreme emotional pain from perceived rejection or criticism

The Emotional Flooding Cycle 1. A trigger occurs (criticism, failure, perceived rejection) 2. Emotion hits at full intensity within seconds 3. Executive function goes offline — can't think clearly 4. Reactive behavior (snapping, withdrawing, spiraling) 5. Shame and self-criticism follow 6. The cycle reinforces itself

Breaking the Cycle The goal isn't to stop feeling emotions — it's to create a gap between the feeling and your response. Even a 10-second pause can be transformative.

content.rawContent: Unhealthy dopamine behaviors are: Easy access, Highly stimulating, Immediately rewarding. Examples: Excessive phone use, Substance use, Overeating, Compulsive behaviors. Healthy dopamine behaviors are: Exercise, Nutritious food, Music, Time in nature, Social connection, Learning, Completing tasks, Creativity, Rest, Sunlight. Frequent overstimulation can reduce sensitivity to everyday rewards. Dopamine supports motivation, attention, pleasure, and learning. The goal is not elimination. The goal is balance. Replace high-intensity habits with steady, supportive ones when possible. Support your nervous system so ordinary life feels rewarding again.
content.styledHtml:
Understanding Healthy vs Unhealthy Dopamine

Unhealthy Dopamine Behaviors

These behaviors typically offer:

  • Easy access
  • Highly stimulating experiences
  • Immediately rewarding sensations

Common examples include:

📱 Excessive phone use
🍷 Substance use
🍕 Overeating
🔄 Compulsive behaviors

Healthy Dopamine Behaviors

These activities provide sustainable, nourishing rewards that support your wellbeing:

🏃‍♀️
Exercise
🥗
Nutritious food
🎵
Music
🌳
Time in nature
👥
Social connection
📚
Learning
✅
Completing tasks
🎨
Creativity
💤
Rest
☀️
Sunlight

The Science Behind Balance

Dopamine is a crucial neurotransmitter that supports motivation, attention, pleasure, and learning. When we frequently overstimulate our dopamine system, we can reduce our sensitivity to everyday rewards, making ordinary life feel less satisfying.

⚖️

The goal is not elimination.

The goal is balance.

By replacing high-intensity habits with steady, supportive ones when possible, we can help our nervous system recalibrate. This allows ordinary life to feel rewarding and fulfilling again.

Support your nervous system so ordinary life feels rewarding again.

content.rawContent:
Unhealthy vs. Healthy Dopamine

Unhealthy Dopamine
• Tolerance builds. Too much stimulation can make the brain less sensitive to dopamine.
• Things feel less rewarding. Activities that once felt fun may start to feel dull.
• The brain wants more stimulation. This can lead to constantly seeking stronger or faster rewards.

Healthy Dopamine
• Built through effort. Exercise and meaningful work create dopamine gradually.
• Supports long-term motivation. These activities help build consistency and mental strength.
• Improves the reward system. Regular exercise can help the brain respond to dopamine in a healthier way.
content.styledHtml:
Unhealthy vs. Healthy Dopamine Cont.

⚠️ Unhealthy Dopamine

  • •Tolerance builds. Too much stimulation can make the brain less sensitive to dopamine.
  • •Things feel less rewarding. Activities that once felt fun may start to feel dull.
  • •The brain wants more stimulation. This can lead to constantly seeking stronger or faster rewards.

✨ Healthy Dopamine

  • •Built through effort. Exercise and meaningful work create dopamine gradually.
  • •Supports long-term motivation. These activities help build consistency and mental strength.
  • •Improves the reward system. Regular exercise can help the brain respond to dopamine in a healthier way.

Remember: Building healthy dopamine patterns takes time, but each small step strengthens your brain's natural reward system. You're training your brain to find satisfaction in meaningful activities!

PART 8: STEADY WORK

Observe your activation patterns throughout the week. Pay attention to when activation feels easy and when it feels difficult. Notice what interferes with your ability to get started and what helps you move forward.

Key areas to observe:
• When activation is easy
• When activation is difficult  
• What interferes with activation
• What helps with activation

Track these patterns without judgment. Remember, steady awareness builds steady change. The goal is simply to notice and understand your unique patterns.

Complete your steady work, watch any assigned videos, and return ready to refine your understanding of your activation patterns.
content.styledHtml:
PART 8: STEADY WORK

Building Awareness Through Gentle Observation

This week, your steady work is to observe your activation patterns with curiosity and compassion. No judgment, just awareness.

What to Observe

✨ When activation is easy

Notice the conditions, feelings, and circumstances that make starting tasks feel natural

🌊 When activation is difficult

Observe what makes getting started feel challenging or overwhelming

🚧 What interferes

Notice the barriers, distractions, or internal states that block activation

🌟 What helps

Identify the supports, strategies, or conditions that facilitate getting started

Track patterns without judgment.

Steady awareness builds steady change.

Your Action Steps

  • Complete your steady work observations throughout the week
  • Watch any assigned videos to deepen your understanding
  • Return to your next session ready to refine your insights

Remember: This is about building awareness, not perfection. Every observation is valuable data for your steady journey.

content.instructions: Set 1-2 SMART goals for improving your time management this week. Be specific and realistic — small wins build momentum.

content.styledHtml:
Time blindness is one of ADHD's most disruptive symptoms. It's not that you don't care about being on time — it's that your brain processes time differently.

What Time Blindness Looks Like

  • Consistently underestimating how long things take
  • Losing track of time during engaging activities
  • The 'just one more thing' trap before leaving
  • Difficulty sensing the passage of time without external cues
  • Living in 'now' and 'not now' — no in-between

The ADHD Time Horizon Neurotypical brains can hold the future in mind while acting in the present. The ADHD brain lives primarily in the present moment. This makes long-term planning feel abstract and deadlines feel unreal until they're imminent.

Making Time Visible The key strategy for time blindness is making time *visible and external*:

  • Analog clocks (you can see time moving)
  • Visual timers (Time Timer, hourglass)
  • Calendar blocking (time has a physical space)
  • Transition alarms (not just one — a sequence)

The Planning Paradox People with ADHD often avoid planning because rigid plans feel suffocating and inevitably fail. The solution isn't more planning — it's *flexible structure*. Think guardrails, not train tracks.

content.rawContent: Emotion regulation is an executive function that is affected in ADHD. People with ADHD struggle to regulate their emotions and it shows up in different ways: Big emotional reactions, Brief but intense feelings, Quickly distracting away from feelings, Avoiding emotional topics, Going from calm to overwhelmed quickly, Noticing what we feel without immediately acting on it, Difficulty with self-activation, Forgetting to revisit unresolved feelings, Strain in relationships, Difficulty keeping feelings hidden, Losing grasp on a programmed task (ex: interpersonal conflict takes all of your energy).
content.styledHtml:
Emotion Regulation and ADHD

Emotion regulation is an executive function that is affected in ADHD. People with ADHD often experience unique challenges in managing their emotional responses.

What Emotional Dysregulation Can Look Like:

🌊 Big emotional reactions that feel disproportionate to the situation

⚡ Brief but intense feelings that come and go quickly

🎯 Quickly distracting away from uncomfortable emotions

🚪 Avoiding emotional topics or difficult conversations

🎢 Going from calm to overwhelmed in what feels like seconds

🤔 Difficulty noticing feelings without immediately acting on them

⚡ Difficulty with self-activation when feeling stuck emotionally

💭 Forgetting to revisit unresolved emotional experiences

💔 Strain in relationships due to emotional intensity or avoidance

🎭 Difficulty keeping feelings hidden when you need to

🎯 Losing focus on tasks when emotional conflicts arise

For example: An interpersonal conflict can completely drain your energy and derail your planned activities

💡 Remember: These challenges are a natural part of how ADHD affects your brain. Recognizing these patterns is the first step toward developing more effective emotional regulation strategies.

cards.body: Whatever you think a task will take, multiply by 1.5 to 2x. ADHD brains consistently underestimate time. Build this buffer into every plan.
cards.title: Time Multiplier Rule

cards.body: Start from the deadline and work backwards. If you need to leave at 9:00, and getting ready takes 45 min (ADHD-adjusted), you start at 8:15. Add 15 min buffer = 8:00 alarm.
cards.title: Reverse Planning

cards.body: Each morning, choose only 3 important tasks. Not 10 — three. If you finish all 3, that's a great day. This prevents the overwhelm of endless to-do lists.
cards.title: The Daily Big 3

cards.body: Set 3 alarms: 15 minutes before (heads up), 5 minutes before (start wrapping up), and at the time (go now). One alarm isn't enough — your brain will dismiss it.
cards.title: Transition Alarms

cards.body: Every Sunday (or Monday morning), spend 20 minutes looking at your week. Block your peak performance windows, add buffer time, and identify the week's Big 3 priorities.
cards.title: Weekly Planning Ritual

content.rawContent:
Regulation Requires Practice

Emotion regulation skills improve with repetition. Not every strategy works for every person. Patience is part of the process.

Examples of Regulating Activities

Regulation often involves movement, rhythm, focus, or sensory grounding:

• Physical activity (running, martial arts, sports)
• Instrumental music
• Cleaning or organizing
• Crossing items off a task list
• Playing an instrument
• Meditation or mindfulness
• Repetitive skill practice
• Time outdoors

Regulation is individual. Identify what settles your nervous system.

What Regulated Feels Like

• Steady breathing and heart rate
• Emotional baseline
• Appropriate stimulation level
• Ability to notice feelings without immediately acting on them
• Reduced impulsivity
• A pause between stimulus and response

The goal is to widen that pause.

Recognizing the Need to Regulate
content.styledHtml:
Practice Noticing: Early Awareness Increases Choice

The earlier you recognize dysregulation, the more tools you have available to respond thoughtfully.

Signs to Watch For

Internal Body Cues

  • Tight chest or shallow breathing
  • Heat rising or flushed feeling
  • Restlessness or fidgeting
  • Sudden impulses or urges

External Observable Changes

  • Changes in tone of voice or posture
  • Different reactions from others around you
  • Awareness of your known personal triggers

Common ADHD Dysregulators

Understanding what commonly triggers dysregulation helps you prepare and respond more skillfully:

  • Interruptions during hyperfocus — sudden breaks in deep concentration
  • Multiple people talking at once — overwhelming auditory input
  • Loud or chaotic environments — sensory overload situations
  • Technology issues — frustrating technical problems
  • Driving stress — traffic, navigation, or time pressure
  • Perceived unfairness — situations that feel unjust
  • Repetition or over-explaining — having to repeat yourself multiple times
  • Feeling misunderstood — communication breakdowns

💡 Key Insight: Identifying triggers allows preparation. When you know what tends to dysregulate you, you can plan coping strategies in advance.

Understanding Impulsivity

Impulsivity in ADHD can show up as:

  • Acting before thinking things through
  • Moving quickly toward immediate rewards
  • Making emotion-driven decisions
  • Having automatic reactions
  • Interrupting conversations
  • Finishing someone's sentences

Remember This

The skill is not suppression.

The skill is slowing.

items.description: Identify What Dysregulates You — List situations, people, or triggers that make you feel off-balance. Rate each from 1-10 (10 = most dysregulating).
Notice Your Common Impulses — What actions or urges often show up when you're stressed or dysregulated?
How Do You Cope with RSD? — Where does Rejection Sensitive Dysphoria show up for you?

Early Messages About Your ADHD — List messages you remember receiving about your ADHD symptoms. Who sent these messages? (Mom, Dad, Brother, Sister, Aunt, Uncle, Cousin, Teacher, Friend, Partner, Tutor, Coach, Other). How do you cope with these early messages now?
Childhood Challenges — How did your ADHD symptoms make life harder when you were young?
Inner Critic — What does your inner critic say? How does it make you feel?
Strengths & Weaknesses — List your strengths and weaknesses.
Throughout the week, notice a moment when your inner critic spoke up. Write what it said and how it made you feel.

modules.title: Daily Routines & Habits
modules.subtitle: Week 5
modules.summary: Build sustainable morning and evening routines, and learn why habit-building with ADHD requires a different approach.

content.styledHtml:
Routines are the scaffolding that holds daily life together — and they're especially important for ADHD brains that struggle with executive function. But here's the catch: traditional habit advice often fails for ADHD.

Why Standard Habit Advice Fails

  • "Just do it every day at the same time" — requires consistent time perception and self-initiation
  • "It takes 21 days to form a habit" — ADHD brains may need 60-90 days, and the habit can still break
  • "Use willpower" — ADHD is literally an executive function deficit; willpower is executive function
  • "Start with one small habit" — ADHD brains often need a cluster of habits that form a chain

The ADHD Routine Principles 1. Chain, don't isolate — Link habits in a sequence so each one triggers the next 2. Externalize the cue — Use alarms, visual reminders, and physical placement instead of relying on memory 3. Reduce decisions — The fewer choices in your routine, the better (lay out clothes, prep coffee, pre-plan meals) 4. Allow imperfection — A 70% routine done consistently beats a 100% routine abandoned after a week 5. Build in dopamine — Pair boring routine steps with something enjoyable (podcast while making lunch, music while cleaning up)

items.text: Wake up at consistent time (within 30-min window)
Hydrate immediately — water bottle by bedside
Take medication (if applicable) — paired with water
Review today's Big 3 tasks (written the night before)
Get dressed (clothes pre-selected the night before)
Quick 10-minute tidy of main living area

items.description: HINDSIGHT (Success) — Think about something in your past that worked out well. What was it? How exactly did you do that? How could you repeat the success? What helped it work? Were there any barriers? How did you get through them?
HINDSIGHT (Learning from a tough moment) — Think of a difficult moment. What went wrong? What could you try differently next time? What got in the way?

HINDSIGHT + SELF-AWARENESS — Notice your patterns. Identify one pattern you see in your behavior. Do you react the same way in certain situations? Are you usually grumpy in the morning? More impulsive with certain people? More irritable in certain places? What kinds of things do you usually look forward to?

Foresight and Time Horizon — Look in your STEADY SYSTEM and identify an upcoming event. Imagine the event using your senses. How might this feel for me? Should I schedule this if I might be tired after work? Will I enjoy going? Should I bring anything I might need later? Who will be there? Will it be loud or overstimulating? Do I actually enjoy doing this activity? Is this important for me to attend?

ADHD + RELATIONSHIPS — How does ADHD show up in your relationships? What changes might you make? How will you make these changes? Consider: Co-workers, Friends, Romantic partner(s), Family, Roommate, Strangers.
Describe yourself in 3 sentences.

modules.title: Productivity & Task Management
modules.subtitle: Week 6
modules.summary: Build an ADHD-friendly task management system and learn strategies for tackling overwhelm, procrastination, and project completion.

content.styledHtml:
Adults with ADHD are often incredibly productive — in bursts. The challenge isn't a lack of capability; it's *sustaining* output and managing the space between bursts.

Common ADHD Productivity Traps

  • The Perfectionism Trap — Can't start because it won't be perfect
  • The Overwhelm Spiral — So much to do that you do nothing
  • The Shiny Object Problem — Starting many projects, finishing few
  • The All-or-Nothing Pattern — Hyperfocused sprint → burnout → avoidance → guilt → repeat

The ADHD Task Management System Forget complex apps with tags, contexts, and due dates on everything. ADHD-friendly task management is: 1. Visible — If it's out of sight, it's gone. Use physical whiteboards, sticky notes, or a single-page dashboard. 2. Simple — One list, one place. Not 5 apps syncing across devices. 3. Forgiving — Uncompleted tasks roll forward without judgment. 4. Dopamine-aware — Mix hard and easy tasks. Start with a quick win to build momentum. 5. Time-anchored — Don't just list tasks; estimate time and assign to specific time blocks.

cards.body: When a big task feels overwhelming, don't try to eat the whole thing. Poke holes in it — do any small piece you can (5 min of research, write one paragraph, make one phone call). The holes add up.
cards.title: Swiss Cheese Method

cards.body: Match task difficulty to your energy level. High energy → creative/complex work. Medium energy → meetings and collaboration. Low energy → admin, organizing, easy tasks.
cards.title: Energy Matching

cards.body: At the end of each day, write down everything you DID accomplish — not just what's on your to-do list. ADHD brains discount their accomplishments. A done list builds evidence of competence.
cards.title: The Done List

cards.body: ADHD brains activate for urgency. Create it: tell someone you'll send them something by Tuesday. Use commitment devices. Schedule an accountability check-in.
cards.title: Artificial Deadlines

cards.body: Break every task into steps small enough that each one takes under 15 minutes. 'Do taxes' becomes 'gather W-2', 'open TurboTax', 'enter income section.' Small steps feel doable.
cards.title: Task Decomposition

items.description: Set up your ADHD-friendly task system: choose ONE tool (whiteboard, paper planner, or simple app) and migrate all floating tasks to it.
Practice the Daily Big 3 method every morning this week.
Keep a 'Done List' every evening — write down at least 5 things you accomplished, no matter how small.
Which productivity trap resonated most with you? What's one pattern you want to break?

modules.title: Relationships & Communication
modules.subtitle: Week 7
modules.summary: Navigate how ADHD affects your relationships and learn communication strategies for both personal and professional settings.

content.styledHtml:
ADHD doesn't just affect you — it affects everyone around you. Understanding how ADHD shows up in relationships is key to building stronger connections.

Common Relationship Challenges

  • Forgetting commitments — Not because you don't care, but because working memory failed
  • Emotional reactivity — Snapping under stress, then feeling terrible about it
  • Conversational tangents — Interrupting, losing track of the topic, or zoning out
  • Inconsistent follow-through — Enthusiastic promises that fade when the novelty wears off
  • The 'parent-child dynamic' — Partners who take on a managing/reminding role

Communication Strategies

  • Admit the pattern, not just the incident — Instead of "Sorry I forgot," try "I know I have a pattern of forgetting things. Here's what I'm doing about it."
  • Use external systems, not promises — "I'll remember" is unreliable. "I've set a reminder" is a system.
  • Ask for direct communication — Hints and subtext are hard for ADHD brains processing at full speed. Ask people to be direct with you.
  • Schedule important conversations — Don't ambush yourself or others with big talks. Pick a time when you have energy and focus.

cards.body: After an ADHD-related conflict, use this sequence: Acknowledge the impact (not just intent), take responsibility for the pattern, share what you're doing differently, and ask what they need.
cards.title: The Repair Ritual

cards.body: When someone is talking to you about something important: put your phone away, make eye contact, and repeat back what you heard. It takes effort, but it shows respect.
cards.title: Active Listening Mode

cards.body: Schedule a 15-minute weekly check-in with your partner/close person. Ask: What's working? What needs attention? What can I help with? Prevents small issues from becoming big ones.
cards.title: The Weekly Check-In

cards.body: When appropriate, explain your ADHD to people who matter: 'I have ADHD, which means [specific thing]. It's not an excuse, but it helps explain [pattern]. Here's how you can help.'
cards.title: ADHD Disclosure

items.description: Practice active listening in at least 2 conversations this week. Afterward, reflect on how it felt.
If you have a partner or close friend, schedule a brief check-in using the Weekly Check-In format.
How has ADHD affected your most important relationships? What's one communication pattern you'd like to change?

modules.title: Sustaining Progress & Next Steps
modules.subtitle: Week 8
modules.summary: Reflect on your growth, consolidate your strategies, and build a long-term plan for living well with ADHD.

content.styledHtml:
Congratulations — you've made it through 8 weeks of dedicated work on understanding and managing your ADHD. That takes real commitment, especially for a brain that thrives on novelty and struggles with sustained effort.

What You've Built Over these weeks, you've developed:

  • A deeper understanding of your ADHD brain and its patterns
  • A personal toolkit of focus, emotional regulation, and time management strategies
  • Morning and evening routines that support your daily functioning
  • A task management system that works with your brain
  • Communication strategies for your relationships
  • Self-compassion and reframing skills

The Maintenance Mindset Here's the truth about ADHD management: it's not a destination. There will be great weeks and hard weeks. Strategies that work now may need refreshing in 6 months. The skills you've learned aren't a cure — they're tools. And tools need maintenance.

The Three Pillars of Long-Term Success 1. Self-awareness — Keep noticing your patterns. Journal, reflect, check in with yourself. 2. External structure — Keep using your systems. When they break (and they will), rebuild them without judgment. 3. Support — Stay connected to your clinician, support groups, or accountability partners. ADHD management is easier with others.
